metadata.subject: Science
metadata.description: Science for Citizens 11 is a practical, applied science course designed to develop scientific literacy and decision-making skills for everyday life, work, and community engagement. Students explore how scientific knowledge informs personal and public health decisions, workplace safety, technology use, and responses to natural hazards. First Peoples traditional knowledge and practices are integrated throughout as legitimate and valuable scientific and practical frameworks.
metadata.general_description: An applied science course developing scientific literacy for daily life, personal and public health, workplace safety, technology literacy, and environmental awareness — connecting science to real-world decisions, local communities, and global challenges.

big_ideas.statement: Scientific processes and knowledge inform decisions and impact daily lives.
big_ideas.elaboration: The ability to evaluate evidence, distinguish reliable from misleading scientific claims, and apply scientific reasoning to personal decisions is a foundational life skill — relevant to health, safety, consumption, and civic participation.

big_ideas.statement: Scientific knowledge develops procedures, techniques, and technologies with workplace implications.
big_ideas.elaboration: Scientific principles underlie the tools, materials, and safety practices of virtually every workplace — from food service and construction to healthcare and manufacturing. Understanding the science makes workers safer and more effective.

big_ideas.statement: Scientific understanding enables humans to respond and adapt to changes locally and globally.
big_ideas.elaboration: Science equips individuals and communities to understand and respond to natural hazards, climate change, technological disruption, and public health challenges — locally and globally.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate sustained curiosity about scientific topics of personal, local, or global interest; make observations to identify questions; formulate multiple hypotheses and predict outcomes.
i_can_statements: I can identify scientific questions that connect to my own life, health, and community.
I can make observations about the world around me and turn them into testable questions.
I can formulate hypotheses about everyday phenomena and predict what will happen based on prior knowledge.

competencies.raw: Collaboratively plan and select appropriate investigation methods; assess risks and ethical issues; use SI units and equipment to collect reliable data; apply accuracy and precision concepts.
i_can_statements: I can plan a practical investigation to answer an everyday scientific question.
I can identify safety and ethical risks in scientific and workplace contexts.
I can collect reliable data using appropriate tools and SI units in real-world settings.

categories.name: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information
categories.raw: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information

competencies.raw: Use local and First Peoples knowledge; analyze patterns and trends; construct and interpret graphs and models; draw evidence-based conclusions; analyze cause-and-effect relationships.
i_can_statements: I can apply First Peoples traditional knowledge and local ecological knowledge to enrich my scientific understanding.
I can analyze patterns and trends in data relevant to health, safety, and environmental decisions.
I can interpret graphs, charts, and data displays used in news media and workplace contexts.
I can draw evidence-based conclusions and explain cause-and-effect relationships in real-world scenarios.

competencies.raw: Evaluate methods and identify error sources; describe improvements; assess model validity; demonstrate informed skepticism; consider implications; analyze source validity.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate the quality of scientific evidence and identify whether a claim is well-supported.
I can exercise informed skepticism — asking what evidence exists for a claim and who is making it.
I can assess whether a source is reliable, biased, or misleading.
I can consider the social, ethical, and environmental implications of scientific and technological decisions.

competencies.raw: Contribute to community care through collaborative approaches; design projects with local and global applications; solve real-world problems; implement multiple strategies.
i_can_statements: I can apply scientific understanding to solve practical problems in daily life and the workplace.
I can design a community-based project that addresses a real local or global challenge.
I can contribute to the well-being of myself, others, and my community using scientific knowledge.

competencies.raw: Formulate theoretical models; communicate scientific ideas with evidence-based arguments; express diverse perspectives through place-based learning.
i_can_statements: I can communicate scientific ideas clearly using appropriate vocabulary for a general audience.
I can present evidence-based arguments about real-world science issues.
I can express how local environments and First Peoples perspectives shape my understanding of science and community.

areas.name: Evidence-Based Decision Making and Scientific Literacy
areas.raw: Scientific processes and knowledge informing decisions.
topics: The nature of science: scientific method, laws, theories, and limitations
Evaluating scientific evidence: peer review, sample size, correlation vs. causation
Identifying misleading claims: pseudoscience, misinformation, and advertising
Science in the media: interpreting headlines, statistics, and data graphics
First Peoples knowledge as legitimate evidence: traditional ecological knowledge and oral tradition

areas.name: Personal and Public Health
areas.raw: Scientific knowledge applied to personal and public health practices.
topics: Nutrition science: macronutrients, micronutrients, food labels, and dietary guidelines
Allergies and intolerances: immune responses, anaphylaxis, and management
Medications: how drugs work, dosage, side effects, and interactions
Vaccinations: immune system function, herd immunity, and public health
First Peoples traditional medicine: plants, practices, and holistic health perspectives
Outdoor safety: hypothermia, altitude sickness, UV exposure, and wilderness first aid

areas.name: Technology and Safety
areas.raw: Scientific knowledge developing procedures and technologies with workplace implications.
topics: Electrical safety: circuits, hazards, and protective devices in home and workplace
Chemical safety: WHMIS, hazardous materials, and safe handling
Workplace safety regulations: WorkSafeBC, safety certifications (FOODSAFE, CPR, First Aid)
Transportation technologies: safety systems, fuel types, and emissions
Health technologies: medical imaging, diagnostic tools, and assistive technologies
Media and communication technologies: digital literacy and privacy

areas.name: Practical Science Applications
areas.raw: Scientific knowledge applied in workplace and materials science contexts.
topics: Basic circuits and electrical systems: practical wiring and safety
Hydraulics and fluid dynamics: applications in plumbing, brakes, and machinery
Materials science: metallurgy, wood, plastics, and composites in trades
Forensic science: evidence collection, fingerprinting, DNA, and blood type
Food science: fermentation, preservation, and chemistry in cooking and food production
Petroleum and industrial processes: refining, safety, and environmental impact

areas.name: Environmental Awareness and Sustainability
areas.raw: Scientific understanding enabling humans to respond and adapt to local and global changes.
topics: Natural hazards: earthquakes, volcanoes, floods, wildfire — causes and preparedness
Natural resource use: renewable and non-renewable resources in BC and globally
Climate change: greenhouse gases, impacts, and personal and community responses
Human impact on ecosystems: pollution, habitat loss, and biodiversity loss
Sustainable living: reducing consumption, waste, and ecological footprint
Indigenous-led sustainability: stewardship practices and land-based knowledge
